content: GBS diagnosis uses Brighton Criteria (levels 1-4 diagnostic certainty). Key features: bilateral flaccid limb weakness, decreased/absent reflexes, monophasic course, progression over ≤4 weeks. Supportive: mild sensory symptoms, cranial nerve involvement (facial diplegia common), autonomic dysfunction, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation (protein >0.55 g/L, WBC <50/μL—WBC >50 suggests alternative diagnosis like HIV, Lyme, sarcoid), NCS showing demyelination (prolonged distal latencies, slow conduction velocities, conduction block, prolonged F-waves) or axonal patterns. Anti-ganglioside antibodies (GM1, GD1a) support axonal variants. MRI spine may show cauda equina enhancement. Differential diagnosis: transverse myelitis (sensory level, bladder/bowel early, MRI cord lesion), botulism (descending paralysis, pupillary involvement), tick paralysis (ascending weakness, remove tick resolves), poliomyelitis (asymmetric, fever, CSF pleocytosis), porphyria (abdominal pain, psychiatric symptoms), myasthenia gravis (fluctuating, ocular/bulbar prominent, normal reflexes), critical illness neuropathy/myopathy. Miller Fisher variant: triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia; anti-GQ1b antibody positive in >90%.
